coredata.dc:title: Dual-color graphene quantum dots and carbon nanoparticles biosensing platform combined with Exonuclease III-assisted signal amplification for simultaneous detection of multiple DNA targets
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Song
Kang, Guangjie
Cui, Fangli
Zhang, Yingwei
coredata.dc:description: Sensitive and simultaneous detection of multiple biomarkers such as target DNA or proteins using biocompatible materials with good analysis performance remains an important challenge. Herein, we successfully developed a signal “off-on” highly sensitive multiplex detection platform based on the combination of dual-color graphene quantum dots (blue GQDs and green GQDs) modified DNA probes with carbon nanoparticles (CNPs), which is a cheap, effective nonfluorescent quencher to simultaneously quench the fluorescence of both GQDs-DNA probes. The Exo III-assisted sequence-independent target recycling and signal amplification strategy was integrated into this sensing platform, which endows it with high sensitivity towards the multiplex detection of targets DNA. The detection limits of 6.6 pM for HIV and 9.5 pM for HBV were achieved respectively, which is about 60-fold lower than that of traditional unamplified homogeneous fluorescent assay methods. Our proposed multiplex detecting platform is advantageous in both respective and simultaneous detection of multiple targets and can also discriminate perfectly matched targets from mismatched targets in both PBS buffer and 1% human serum samples, demonstrating its potential to be a reliable strategy for highly sensitive simultaneous detection of multiple target genes in practical diagnosis applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Simultaneous detection
Multiple targets
Dual-color GQDs-DNA probes
Exonuclease III
Fluorescent signal amplification

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of GQDs modified single-strand DNA probes, bGQDs-PHIV and gGQDs-PHBV
Detecting the DNA sequences of the two targets virus separately
Simultaneous detection of the multiplex targets
Discrimination of the single-base mismatched targets in PBS and human serum sample
The design strategy of this multiplex detection system
Characterizations of GQDs before and after DNA modification, and CNPs
Verifying the feasibility of Exo III-assisted target recycling and signal amplification strategy in single-target detection system
Optimizing the experimental parameters of the fluorescence biosensing platform
Respective detection of THIV and THBV in the dual-color probes biosensing platform
Simultaneous detection of multiplex targets, THIV and THBV, using this EXO III-assisted dual-color probes biosensing platform
The selectivity and anti-interference ability of the GQDs-ssDNA probes and CNPs biosensing platform

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the simultaneous detection process of multiplex targets (THIV and THBV) by using dual-color GQDs-DNA probes and CNPs nanoplatform with EXO III-assisted target recycling and signal amplification strategy. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Characterization of the proposed biosensor. TEM images of the two kinds of GQDs and the corresponding size distributions of (A, B) bGQDs and (C, D) gGQDs. (E, F) TEM images of CNPs and GQDs-CNPs. The rectangular areas label the GQDs-ssDNA probes adsorbed on the surfaces of CNPs.

Fluorescence spectra of (A) bGQDs-PHIV probes system and (B) gGQDs-PHBV probes system at their respective excitation wavelength of 315 nm and 365 nm: (a) the original GQDs-ssDNA probes solution; (b) the fluorescence quenching after the addition of CNPs; (c) the fluorescence recovery after adding target DNA; (d) the enhanced fluorescence recovery after adding target DNA and 20 U Exo III.

The fluorescence spectra of the dual-color probes and CNPs mixture assay system after adding different concentrations of target THIV in the presence of EXO III. (A) The fluorescence recovery after adding target THIV of 0–10 nM and (B) the relationship between fluorescence recovery rate of F/F0 and target THIV concentration, with error bar from at least 3 independent experiments (F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity of the dual-color probes and CNPs mixture at 430 nm in the presence and absence of THIV, respectively). Inset shows the linear response relationship at lower target concentration ranges. The excitation wavelength and emission wavelength are 315 nm and 430 nm, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

The fluorescence spectra of the dual-color probes and CNPs mixture assay system after adding different concentrations of target THBV in the presence of EXO III. (A) the fluorescence recovery after adding target THBV of 0–14 nM and (B) the relationship between fluorescence recovery rate of F/F0 and target THBV concentration, with error bar from at least 3 independent experiments (F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity of the dual-color probes and CNPs mixture at 514 nm in the presence and absence of THBV, respectively). Inset shows the linear response relationship at lower target concentration ranges. The excitation wavelength and emission wavelength are 365 nm and 514 nm, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Fluorescence spectra of the double-color GQDs-ssDNA probes and CNPs platform assay system upon the simultaneous addition of target THIV and THBV in the presence of EXO III. (A) the fluorescence recovery after adding the two targets mixture with different concentrations. (B) and (C) the relationship between fluorescence recovery intensity and target concentration, with error bar from 3 independent experiments. Inset shows the linear response relationship at lower target concentration range. The excitation wavelength is 340 nm, and the emission wavelength are 435 nm and 506 nm, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Selectivity assessment of this GQDs-ssDNA probes and CNPs biosensing platform for the detection of complementary targets in (A) HIV and (B) HBV system against noncomplementary rDNA and single-base mismatched targets MHIV/MHBV in PBS buffer (cyan) and 1% human serum samples (purple). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
The DNA sequences used in the experiments are given as follows.

head.ce:title: Dual-color graphene quantum dots and carbon nanoparticles biosensing platform combined with Exonuclease III-assisted signal amplification for simultaneous detection of multiple DNA targets

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing, 100082, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering
College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing, 100082, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Song Wang, Guangjie Kang and Fangli Cui contributed equally to the work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sensitive and simultaneous detection of multiple biomarkers such as target DNA or proteins using biocompatible materials with good analysis performance remains an important challenge. Herein, we successfully developed a signal “off-on” highly sensitive multiplex detection platform based on the combination of dual-color graphene quantum dots (blue GQDs and green GQDs) modified DNA probes with carbon nanoparticles (CNPs), which is a cheap, effective nonfluorescent quencher to simultaneously quench the fluorescence of both GQDs-DNA probes. The Exo III-assisted sequence-independent target recycling and signal amplification strategy was integrated into this sensing platform, which endows it with high sensitivity towards the multiplex detection of targets DNA. The detection limits of 6.6 pM for HIV and 9.5 pM for HBV were achieved respectively, which is about 60-fold lower than that of traditional unamplified homogeneous fluorescent assay methods. Our proposed multiplex detecting platform is advantageous in both respective and simultaneous detection of multiple targets and can also discriminate perfectly matched targets from mismatched targets in both PBS buffer and 1% human serum samples, demonstrating its potential to be a reliable strategy for highly sensitive simultaneous detection of multiple target genes in practical diagnosis applications.

Simultaneous detection of multiplex targets (THIV and THBV) by using dual-color GQDs-DNA probes and CNPs nanoplatform with EXO III-assisted target recycling and signal amplification strategy.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: The development of fluorescence detection techniques, especially the emerging nucleic acid-based methods, have provided powerful tools for disease diagnosis and treatment [1,2]. However, the current reports are mostly focused on single-target detection, and in practical physiological process, the occurrence of diseases may be relevant to multiple genes or biomarkers [3,4]. The simultaneous multiplex detection of various specific targets would increase the accuracy and reliability of disease diagnosis and also contribute to the reductions of time and cost. Therefore, it is still a significant challenge to develop novel biosensing method to realize the simultaneous detection of multiple targets with high sensitivity and selectivity.

Currently, a lot of research work have been done to explore the multiplex detection strategies [3–10]. However, in most of these assays, one capture probe normally binds with only one target molecule and this binding ratio of 1:1 limits the possibility of signal amplification, leading to the low sensitivity of the assays. In order to realize the accurate detection of target with low abundance, it requires an efficient amplification strategy to be integrated into the design of detection system. Although polymerase chain reaction (PCR) is the most classical target amplification method and the gold standard for DNA detection, it often requires specific and expensive equipment with time-consuming thermal recycling procedures [11]. Among the isothermal amplification strategies, Exo-III assisted amplification strategy has its unique advantages and character, sequence-independent nucleic amplification. Exonuclease III (Exo III), one kind of widely-employed sequence-independent enzyme, is not active on 3′-overhang ends of double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) and single-stranded DNA (ssDNA), but can only catalyze the stepwise removal of mononucleotides from 3′-hydroxyl termini of dsDNA with blunt or recessed 3′- termini. Through rational design, Exo-III assisted strategy can be applied to the amplified detection of various targets [12–17]. For example, Tan et al. developed an Exo III-assisted autocatalytic target recycling amplification strategy for highly sensitive and rapid screening of specific oligonucleotide sequences using three dye-labeled DNA sequences, which is about 50-fold more sensitive than traditional unamplified homogeneous biosensors [17].

It should be noted that the most frequently used fluorophores in the homogeneous assays include organic dyes and some inorganic fluorescent nanoparticles, which have been well developed for a period of time, but still have some inevitable drawbacks. Organic dyes fluorophores usually require a sophisticated synthetic process as well as tedious modifying steps for conjugating with DNA strands and suffer from high cost, poor photostability, easy photobleaching and small Stokes shifts [18]. Other inorganic fluorophores, such as semiconductor QDs and metal nanoparticles, also have some drawbacks of high toxicity, relatively high cost and harsh manipulation, meaning that they are not excellent choice as biosensing probes [19,20]. To satisfy the detecting demands of multiplex targets, different colored fluorophores without spectra interference and overlap are required to be simultaneously integrated into the entire detection system for multiple targets. Therefore, exploring biocompatible materials as qualified fluorophore probes to satisfy the various color requirements of multiplex detection system and integrating rational designed signal amplification strategy into detecting system would boost the development of multiplex target biosensing platform. The emerging fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) has sparked intensive research interests duo to their superior performance in terms of excellent photostability, good biological compatibility, low toxicity and resistance to photobleaching properties, compared to conventional organic dyes and other semiconductor QDs [20], thus making them promising candidates in biological imaging, biosensing, drug/gene delivery, and anticancer agents [21–26]. As such, it should be an excellent choice as fluorophores to conjugate with DNA strands [27].

In this work, we have successfully developed an Exo III-assisted cyclic amplification strategy for the simultaneous detection of multiplex DNA using two kinds of different colored GQDs, blue GQDs (bGQDs) and green GQDs (gGQDs), as fluorophores modified on their corresponding ssDNA probes. Besides, in most of fluorescent biosensor systems, a nonfluorescent broad-range quencher is usually necessary for adjusting the signal “on-off” of fluorescence probe, which can also endow the sensing system with high sensitivity. Here, we chose carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) as a nonfluorescent quencher to simultaneously quench the fluorescence of two kinds of GQDs modified probes, since CNPs have the advantages of large specific surface area, good biocompatibility, low-cost and easier to be produced, and has been successfully used as effective fluorescence quencher in our previous work and other researcher’s work [28–33]. By integrating this CNPs nonfluorescent quencher with dual-color GQDs with the assistance of Exo-III sequence-independent signal amplification strategy and choosing two virus DNA (Human immunodeficiency virus HIV, hepatitis B virus HBV) as our model targets, we successfully constructed a signal-on biosensing platform to achieve simultaneous detection of multiplex targets with high sensitivity. This multiplex detection system could also discriminate perfectly matched target DNA from mismatched DNA and have been successfully used for the detection of target DNA in 1% human serum. Our work demonstrated that this Exo III-assisted GQDs-ssDNA probes and CNPs nanoplatform can provide an efficient strategy for simultaneous detection of multiplex targets with high sensitivity and selectivity and could be expected to further expand into other multiplex detection systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental section
Reagents
ce:section.ce:para: The DNA oligonucleotides were purchased from Shanghai Sangon Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All the sequences of DNA strands were listed in Table 1 (the mismatched bases are underlined; perfectly matched sequences are highlighted in bold). 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were purchased from Shanghai Aobo Biotechnology Co. Ltd. Human serum samples were provided by the people’s liberation army 301 general hospital. All the other chemicals were purchased from Beijing Chemical Reagent Co. and used without further purification. The water used throughout all experiments was purified using a Millipore system. The buffer solutions used in all the experiments were prepared as follows: PBS buffer (0.1 M Na2HPO4/NaH2PO4, pH = 7.4, 50 mM NaCl), TE buffer (40 mM Tris, 2 mM EDTA, pH = 7.4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization methods
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies of the synthesized GQDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), performed on an instrument (FEI Tecnai G20 S-Twin) with an accelerating voltage of 200 KV. The UV–visible absorption spectra were measured on a spectrophotometer (U-3900H, Hitachi, Japan) with a variable wavelength between 200 and 600 nm. A fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-7000) with an excitation wavelength of 315 nm and a slit width of 5 nm was used for fluorescence spectra measurements. Fluorescence lifetime was determined using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (FLS980, Edinburgh instruments). Infrared spectra (IR) were collected on an IR spectrometer (Nexus670, Thermo Nicolet Corporation, American) in the wave number range of 700–4000 cm−1 using KBr pellets. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed using a ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of bGQDs and gGQDs
ce:section.ce:para: bGQDs: Graphene oxide (GO) was prepared from graphite by the modified Hummers method [34]. 50 mg GO were oxidized in 98% H2SO4 and 68% HNO3 with a ratio of 3:1 for 2 h under mild ultrasonication (500 W, 40 kHz), then diluted by distilled water. Next, the oxidized products were filtered using a 0.22-μm microporous membrane and re-dispersed in 40 mL of distilled water. 40 mg products were dispersed in DI water (40 mL) with ultrasonication of 3 h and then the pH value was tuned to 10 with NaOH (1 M). The suspension was transferred to a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting black suspension was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane and a brown filter solution. The colloidal solution was further dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 3500 Da) for 3 days. Finally, the powdery bGQDs were obtained after lyophilization.

gGQDs: gGQDs were synthesized from GO powder by using a facile one-step solvothermal method. In a typical synthesis process, 100 mg GO were dispersed in N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) (40 mL) with ultrasonication of 3 h and then the suspension was transferred to a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting black suspension was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane and the brown filter solution was collected, which is the GQDs dispersion in DMF. Then, DMF was removed by concentrating the brown filter solution at 85 °C under reduced pressure with a rotary evaporator. The residual DMF can be removed thoroughly by dialyzing in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 3500 Da) for 3 days. The final gGQDs powder was obtained through lyophilization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQDs modified single-strand DNA probes, bGQDs-PHIV and gGQDs-PHBV
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical conjugation process, the bGQDs powder was firstly dissolved in phosphate buffer (10.0 mL, 0.1 mg/mL, pH 7.4) to obtain bGQDs suspension, and then the pH value of the solution was adjusted to 5 for the protonation of carboxyl groups on the surface of bGQDs. After that, 1 mL of 25 mM EDC and 1 mL of 37.5 mM NHS were added into the above solution to activate the carboxylic group of bGQDs for 30 min at room temperature under stirring. Then the pH value of solution was adjusted to 7.4 and the solution was divided into two equal parts with each one of 6 mL. The bGQDs-PHIV conjugates were obtained by adding 20 μL of PHIV oligonucleotides (100 μM) into the above activated solution and the condensation reaction was allowed to process for 2 h at room temperature with continuous stirring. The gGQDs-PHBV conjugates were obtained in a similar way by adding 20 μL PHBV oligonucleotides (100 μM) into the condensation reaction solution of gGQDs. Finally, two kinds of bGQDs-PHIV and gGQDs-PHBV probes were obtained after centrifugation treatment (8000 rpm, and10 min) and three times of washing procedure with PBS buffer to remove excessive oligonucleotides.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation of carbon nanoparticles (CNPs)
ce:section.ce:para: CNPs were prepared following a previous report: First of all, the obtained 5 mg of carbon soot by smoldering a candle using a well-established method was suspended in 20 mL of a water/ethanol mixture (1:1) with the help of ultrasonication [29,33]. Next, the black solution was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 2 min to remove large carbon soot particles. The supernatant was collected and centrifuged again at 6000 rpm for 2 min. The obtained black precipitate was redispersed in 20 mL of a water/ethanol mixture (1:1) for further characterization and the subsequent application. According to the mass of the final residue, the concentration of the prepared suspension was about 0.3 mg/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detecting the DNA sequences of the two targets virus separately
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, the bGQDs-PHIV probe and gGQDs-PHBV probe were mixed together in a ratio of 2:3, and then CNPs (40 μL, 0.3 mg/mL) was introduced into the probes mixture at room temperature to quench the fluorescence of all probes through at least 40 min of incubation. The different concentrations of target THIV together with 20 U Exo III were added into the above solutions to obtain a final solution volume of 500 μL and incubated for a period of time for the subsequent fluorescence detection. The fluorescence recovery intensity was measured at the emission wavelength of 430 nm by using fluorescence spectrophotometer under 315 nm excitation wavelength. The detecting experiments of target THBV were similar to the above procedure while changing the excitation wavelength to 365 nm and the emission wavelength to 514 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous detection of the multiplex targets
ce:section.ce:para: First, the bGQDs-PHIV probe and the gGQDs-PHBV probe were also mixed in a ratio of 2:3, and then CNPs (40 μL, 0.3 mg/mL) was introduced into the mixture at room temperature to quench the fluorescence of all probes after at least 40 min of incubation. Then, together with 20 U Exo III, different concentrations of target THIV and THBV were added into the above solution for a period of time incubation and the mixture was diluted with PBS buffer to a final volume of 500 μL. The subsequent fluorescence recovery was measured by using fluorescence spectrophotometer under 340 nm excitation wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discrimination of the single-base mismatched targets in PBS and human serum sample
ce:section.ce:para: For HIV detection system, the bGQDs-PHIV probes were mixed with 40 μL of CNPs (0.3 mg/mL) at room temperature and incubated for at least 40 min to quench the fluorescence. Then, in the presence of 20 U Exo III, the noncomplementary oligonucleotides (rDNA) or the single-base mismatched targets MHIV or the perfect complementary targets THIV were respectively added into the above mixture to obtain a same concentration of 10 nM and incubate a period of time for fluorescence recovery. The fluorescence recovery intensity caused by these different targets was measured separately under 315 nm excitation wavelength for the discrimination of mismatched targets. The HBV detection system has a similar experimental procedure by adding targets at the concentration of 14 nM. All the above experiments were performed again in 500 μL volume of 1% human serum solution by spiking the buffer with 5 μL of serum sample at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
The design strategy of this multiplex detection system
ce:section.ce:para: The overall concept of our multiplex detection strategy is shown in the scheme of Fig. 1 . First, we choose two DNA sequences (THIV and THBV) as our detection targets from the two-representative virus, the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) and the hepatitis B virus (HBV), respectively. The 5′-end of the two DNA probes (PHIV and PHBV, complementary to target DNA sequences) was labeled with two kinds of fluorophores bGQDs and gGQDs respectively. Then, CNPs could easily adsorb these two kinds of GQDs-ssDNA probes due to π-π stacking interactions between ssDNA bases and CNPs, and efficiently quench their fluorescence simultaneously as a single energy acceptor based on possible Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) from GQDs to CNPs. Next, the addition of two targets DNA sequences will trigger the respective hybridization between the corresponding probes and targets DNA sequences and the formation of dsDNA with a small 3′-overhang end of 4-base in targets DNA sequences. The formed dsDNA would be prone to leave from the surface of CNPs due to the weakening interactions between DNA bases and CNPs, leading to the fluorescence recovery of bGQDs and gGQDs. During this process, the addition of Exo III can catalyze the stepwise digestion of mononucleotides of probes from the blunt 3′ termini of dsDNA, resulting in the release of the targets DNA sequences and GQDs. The released GQDs were no longer quenched by CNPs, thus their fluorescence intensity recovered thoroughly. The recycled targets DNA can initiate the next round of capturing probes and Exo III-assisted cleavage of probes, and the thorough fluorescence recovery of GQDs. This Exo III-assisted sequence-independent recycling of multiple targets DNA can result in significant fluorescence signal amplification of different colored fluorophores to achieve simultaneous detection of multiplex targets at lower concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of GQDs before and after DNA modification, and CNPs
ce:section.ce:para: Before conducting the detection experiments, we first characterized the obtained bGQDs sample from hydrothermal method and the prepared gGQDs sample from solvothermal method. As shown in Fig. 2 , the typical transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of these two kinds of GQDs clearly show their sizes and their good dispersity. We measured the sizes of at least 100 particles in TEM images of each GQDs samples and calculated the statistic distribution of particle size from these measured raw data. The bGQDs sample presents the diameter of around 3 nm with a good size distribution in the range of 2–4 nm (Fig. 2B), and the gGQDs sample shows the diameter of around 4 nm with a size distribution in the ranges of 2–6 nm (Fig. 2D). High resolution TEM (HRTEM) images reveal that both two kinds of GQDs are crystalline with a lattice distance of about 0.24 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite structure (insets of Fig. 2A and C). Fig. S1 present fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of these two kinds of GQDs and their corresponding fluorescent images. The spectra of bGQDs show a pronounced excitation peak at 315 nm and a maximum emission peak at 430 nm which is corresponding to a blue fluorescence, while the gGQDs show the emission wavelength in the range of green fluorescence, with a maximum emission peak at 514 nm and an optimal excitation wavelength at 365 nm. Then, XPS spectra were used to analyze the chemical compositions and chemical states of these two kinds of GQDs (Figs. S2 and S3). The survey XPS spectrum of bGQDs obviously shows the predominated C 1s peak and O 1s peak, with a C/O atomic ratio of ∼2.21, suggesting that this bGQDs are mainly composed of two elements, carbon and oxygen. Furthermore, the presence of C-C/CC, C-O (epoxyl and hydroxyl) and CO (carbonyl and carboxyl) groups could be confirmed based on the three peaks at 284.8, 286.25 and 288.45 eV deconvoluted from the C1s spectra of bGQDs (Fig. S2B). In terms of gGQDs, they are mainly composed of carbon and oxygen from the predominated C1s peak and O1s peak, and a small amount of nitrogen from N1s peak (Fig. S3). Similar like bGQDs, the C 1s spectra of gGQDs could be deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.85, 286.65, and 288.35 eV, associated with C-C/CC, C-O (epoxyl and hydroxyl), and CO (carbonyl and carboxyl) groups, respectively [25]. The O 1s spectra of gGQDs could be deconvoluted into three peaks at 531.25, 2532.1, and 533.1 eV, associated with O-C, OC (epoxyl and carbonyl), and OC-O (carboxyl) groups, respectively. While the N 1s peak at 399.75 eV could be attribute to C-N-C, which should be derived from DMF solvent. The presence of oxygen-containing groups related with carboxyl group on these two kinds of GQDs could contribute to their good water solubility and reaction activity for the subsequent conjugation with amino-modified DNA.

The optical absorption properties of GQDs before and after DNA modification were also studied through UV–vis absorption spectra as shown in Fig. S4. First, the typical absorption peak at ca. 230 nm, together with a relatively weak absorption peak at ca.270 nm was observed in both of bGQDs and gGQDs, which can be attributed to the π-π∗ transition of aromatic sp2 domains and n-π∗ transition of CO bond, respectively. After the modification of ssDNA probes on these two kinds of GQDs, the corresponding GQDs-DNA bioconjugates all present an obvious absorption peak at approximately 260 nm besides the characteristic absorption peak of GQDs at 230 nm, indicating the successful conjugation of GQDs with ssDNA probes. We also used Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectra to check the connection between GQDs and ssDNA probes (Fig. S5). As shown in Fig. S5a, the samples of bGQDs and bGQDs-DNA conjugates all present the characteristic vibration bands at 3049 cm−1, 1604 cm−1 and 1068 cm−1 [25,35,36], attributed to the stretch vibration of C-OH, CC and C-O-C groups, and the skeletal vibration from the graphene domain at 1434 cm−1. Besides, two new emerging vibration peaks can also be observed at 1024 cm−1 and 927 cm−1, associated with the PO and P–O stretching vibration of ssDNA in the sample of bGQDs-DNA conjugates (red line), demonstrating the successful conjugation of bGQDs with ssDNA PHIV probes. As shown in Fig. S5b, we observed similar results in the samples of gGQDs and gGQDs-DNA conjugates and an obvious characteristic stretching vibration peak of amido bond at 1681 cm−1 after the conjugation of ssDNA probes, verifying the successful conjugation of gGQDs with PHBV probes through amidation reaction. Moreover, we investigated the zeta potential of two kinds of GQDs before and after conjugation with amino-DNA, as shown in Table S1. The results indicate that these two kinds of GQDs all presented negative charged surfaces, and more negative charges were introduced after their conjugation with ssDNA probes, attributed to the negatively charged phosphate backbone of ssDNA probes. The above results from FT-IR and Zeta potential data all demonstrated the successful modification of ssDNA probes on the surface of these two kinds of GQDs. The corresponding fluorescence pictures of GQDs-ssDNA probes solutions under the irradiation of 302 nm and 360 nm UV lamp were also presented in Fig. S6, still showing distinct blue and green fluorescence. Furthermore, we also measured the fluorescence quantum yields of bGQDs and gGQDs before and after DNA modification as shown in Table S2. Both GQDs-DNA probes samples still reserved a relatively high fluorescence quantum yields after DNA modification.

In this work, CNPs was prepared by a one-step procedure under mild conditions and used as a nonfluorescence quencher to simultaneously quench the fluorescence of the two kinds of GQDs-DNA probes, since it has the π-rich nature for effectively absorbing DNA probes through π-π stacking interactions between DNA bases and CNPs [21], leading to the possible energy transfer or electron transfer processes for fluorescence quenching. It should be mentioned that we investigated the fluorescence emission spectrum of CNPs and found that the fluorescence of CNPs were almost negligible under the excitation wavelength ranges of the two kinds of GQDs from 315 nm to 365 nm (Fig. S7), indicating that CNPs would not interfere in the fluorescence detection of the current system. The TEM images showed that these CNPs are nearly spherical in the diameter range of 25–50 nm, as shown in Fig. 2E, and the enlarged TEM image in Fig. 2F presented the rectangular areas which label clearly the GQDs-ssDNA probes adsorbed on the surfaces of CNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Verifying the feasibility of Exo III-assisted target recycling and signal amplification strategy in single-target detection system
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the design of our biosensing platform as mentioned in the previous part, this fluorescence biosensing system for any target DNA detection involved three parts: 1) GQDs-ssDNA probes adsorb on the surface of CNPs (fluorescence quenching), 2) target DNA triggered release of GQDs-ssDNA probes from the surface of CNPs (fluorescence recovery), and 3) Exo III-assisted target DNA recycling (enhanced fluorescence recovery). To verify the feasibility of this signal “off-on” Exo III-assisted and target-triggered fluorescence biosensing strategy, we accomplished the fluorescence quenching and recovery experiments, and compared the fluorescence recovery results with and without the presence of Exo III in bGQDs-PHIV probes system and gGQDs-PHBV probes system, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the bGQDs-PHIV probes presents a high fluorescence emission intensity at the wavelength of 430 nm (line a) when excited at 315 nm, and an obvious quenching of fluorescence can be observed as expected after the addition of 40 μL CNPs (line b). Then, the target DNA THIV was introduced into the system in the presence or absence of 20 U Exo III. As shown in line c of Fig. 3A, in the absence of EXO III, only a fluorescence recovery of 11.25% was observed upon the addition of 10 nM target THIV. In contrast, under the same concentration of 10 nM target THIV, the introduction of EXO III could result in an obvious fluorescence recovery of 78.49% (line d), meaning that Exo III-assisted target DNA recycling could trigger the release of much more PHIV probes from the surface of CNPs for enhanced fluorescence recovery and signal amplification. In the detection system of THBV, the gGQDs-PHBV probes presents an optimal fluorescence emission peak at the wavelength of 514 nm (line a) when excited at 365 nm, and a similar situation of quenching and recovery was observed as shown in Fig. 3B. The fluorescence recovery rates were 16.69% and 72.67% respectively, in the absence or presence of 20 U EXO III upon the addition of 14 nM target THBV, confirming again the effective target recycling and the significant signal amplification of fluorescence recovery based on this EXO III-assisted fluorescence biosensing strategy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimizing the experimental parameters of the fluorescence biosensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: Before conducting the multiplex detection using these two kinds of fluorescent GQDs-DNA probes, we need to optimize the experimental parameters of fluorescence detection system. The following parameters should be monitored and optimized, including the adding volume of CNPs for the effective fluorescence quenching and the subsequent fluorescence recovery, the concentration of Exo III used, and the reaction time required for the maximum fluorescence quenching and recovery.

We first optimized the adding amount of CNPs for effective fluorescence quenching for each kind of GQDs-DNA probe respectively. As shown in Fig. S8A, when CNPs was introduced into the bGQDs-PHIV probes solution, the fluorescence emission intensity of the bGQDs-PHIV at 433 nm gradually decreased with the increasing volume of CNPs from 0 to 40 μL. A curve of fluorescence intensity as a function of the adding volume of CNPs was plotted in Fig. S8B. It shows that the decrease of fluorescence intensity of the bGQDs-PHIV reached a saturation point when more than 40 μL of CNPs was added into the solution, and about 84% quenching of the bGQDs-PHIV fluorescence emission intensity was observed in the presence of 40 μL of CNPs, suggesting that almost all the bGQDs-PHIV probes had been adsorbed on the surface of CNPs and the close proximity leading to the subsequent the possible energy transfer or electron transfer processes between GQDs and CNPs for fluorescence quenching. The maximum quenching efficiency of CNPs to the attached bGQDs-PHIV probes was about 87% based on the ratio of the maximum quenching intensity after adding 50–80 μL CNPs over the initial fluorescence intensity of bGQDs-PHIV probes. As shown in Figs. S8C and D, the fluorescence intensity of the gGQDs-PHBV probes solution at 514 nm presented a similar quenching trend as the bGQDs-PHIV probes with the increased volume of CNPs from 0 to 80 μL, and a saturated point of 50 μL in the quenching equilibrium stage also was observed. The maximum quenching efficiency of CNPs to the adsorbed gGQDs-PHBV probes was about 83% by calculating the ratio of the maximum quenching intensity over the initial fluorescence intensity of gGQDs-PHBV probes solution. It should be noted that the excess amount of CNPs would probably adsorb single-strand target DNA, which may lead to a dramatic decrease in the sensitivity, and a less amount of CNPs could lead to inadequate quenching and give rise to a higher background fluorescence signal that may cause a decrease of the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR). Hence, we choose a propriate 40 μL volume of CNPs for the detection experiments of any single-target DNA by using bGQDs-PHIV or gGQDs-PHBV probes in the following experiments. Besides, in order to achieve the best biosensing performance, the concentration of Exo III was optimized as shown in Fig. S9. Experimental results showed that an amount at 20 U of Exo III could provide the maximum fluorescence recovery for the sensing system at a given concentration of target DNA.

To optimize the fluorescence detection conditions, the kinetic processes of fluorescence quenching of the ssDNA probes after the addition of CNPs and the subsequent fluorescence recovery after adding target DNA and Exo III were also studied by measuring the time-dependent fluorescence emission spectra. Fig. S10A shows the fluorescence quenching and fluorescence recovery of the bGQDs-PHIV probes as a function of incubation time. The fluorescence quenching data in black line exhibits a rapid decline in the first 20 min followed by a slow decrease in the subsequent 20 min and reached to the quenching equilibrium after 40 min of incubation with CNPs. The subsequent fluorescence recovery (red line) was also a time-dependent process, and the maximum fluorescence recovery was obtained after 60 min of incubation with target DNA THIV and Exo III. A similar situation also appears in gGQDs-PHBV probes system (Fig. S10B), and the maximum of fluorescence quenching and fluorescence recovery can be achieved at the time point of 40 min and 60 min respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Respective detection of THIV and THBV in the dual-color probes biosensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: Before conducting the simultaneous detection of two different kinds of target DNA, bGQDs-PHIV probes and gGQDs-PHBV probes were mixed in an optimal ratio of 3:2 to form a mixture of dual-color GQDs-ssDNA probes. These dual-color probes mixture show two distinct fluorescence emission peaks at 435 nm and 506 nm when excited at the selected common excitation wavelength of 340 nm (Fig. S11), and these two emission peaks positions are almost in coincidence with that of the previous emission peaks (430 nm and 514 nm) obtained at their respective optimal excitation wavelengths. According to the optimized conditions explored before, CNPs with an optimal volume of 40 μl were added into the probes mixture system to effectively quench their fluorescence. As shown in Fig. S8 (red line), the 74% intensity decreases of both two fluorescence emission peaks could be observed, indicating that both the two GQDs-ssDNA probes were adsorbed onto the surface of CNPs at the same time for effective quenching between probes and CNPs.

Then, we first evaluated the single-target sensing performance of this multiplex detection platform and the EXO III-assisted signal amplification rate. First, different concentrations of target THIV together with 20 U EXO III were added into the system of dual-color probes mixture and CNPs, and the fluorescence intensity was monitored under the excitation wavelength of 315 nm. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the intensity of fluorescence emission peak at 430 nm was obviously increased with the increasing THIV concentration from 0.01 nM to 10 nM, which is corresponding to the release of bGQDs-PHIV probes from CNPs and fluorescence recovery due to the hybridization interaction with Target THIV. Due to the EXO III-assisted target recycling, only a 10 nM concentration of target THIV could lead to the completely fluorescence recovery of bGQDs-PHIV probes. A linear relationship in the fluorescence intensity recovery at 430 nm was observed in the concentration range of 0–1 nM (Fig. 4B), with a linear fitting equation expressed as F/F0 = 1.83x + 1.01 (where F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity of the double-color probes and CNPs mixture at 430 nm in the presence and absence of THIV, respectively), in which R2 = 0.998. The detection limit (LOD) for THIV was determined to be 6.6 pM, calculated according to the 3σ/S, where σ represents the standard deviation of ten blank measurements and S is the slope of the linearity range used. As a control, the experiments of adding different concentrations of Target THIV in the absence of EXO III were also carried out, and a target THIV concentration of 130 nM was at least required to realize the maximum fluorescence recovery of bGQDs-PHIV probes (Fig. S12A). A linear fitting in the plot of fluorescence recovery was observed in the target concentration range of 0–15 nM (Fig. S12B inset), and the detection limit was calculated to be 0.406 nM, which is about 62-fold higher than that of Exo III-assisted target recycling and signal amplification method, demonstrating the advantages of introducing Exo III to improve the sensitivity of this biosensing platform.

In a similar way, different concentrations of target THBV were also added into the mixture of dual-color probes and CNPs for the specific detection, and the fluorescence intensity was monitored under the excitation wavelength of 365 nm and emission wavelength of 514 nm. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence intensity is gradually increased with the continuous addition of THBV, and only 14 nM concentration of target THBV could arise to the maximum fluorescence recovery in the presence of Exo III. Fig. 5B shows that the fluorescence recovery intensity has a linear relationship with the concentration of THBV at the range of 0–2 nM, with a linear fitting equation expressed as F/F0 = 1.49x + 1.001 (where the F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity of the double-color GQDs-ssDNA probes and CNPs mixture in the presence and absence of THBV, respectively), in which R2 = 0.993. The detection limit for THBV in this EXO III-assisted dual-color probe system was calculated to be 9.5 pM. In contrast, the control experiments of adding various concentrations of THBV in the absence of EXO III showed a detection limit of 0.55 nM, with a concentration of at least 180 nM required for the maximum fluorescence recovery (Fig. S13). This Exo III-assisted target recycling and signal amplification strategy present almost 58-folds higher sensitivity than that of normal strategy for THBV detection. All the above results indicate that the introduction of nuclease EXO III remarkably improved the detecting sensitivity of this dual-color probes biosensor, and the detection limits are comparable or better than those of some reported detection systems as shown in Table S3 [37–41].

It should be noted that the addition of any single-target into the mixture of dual-color GQDs-ssDNA probes only resulted in the fluorescence recovery of its corresponding color probe when excited under its optimal excitation wavelength and has negligible impact on the fluorescence intensity of the other color probe, as shown in Fig. S14. It demonstrates that these two kinds of GQDs-ssDNA probes have no interactive interferences and the current dual-color probes system has good distinguishing ability to these two kinds of targets.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous detection of multiplex targets, THIV and THBV, using this EXO III-assisted dual-color probes biosensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, the simultaneous detection of the two targets THIV and THBV in the presence of EXO III was also carried out under the excitation wavelength of 340 nm. The fluorescence intensity of the two distinct fluorescence emission peaks at 435 nm and 506 nm excited at 340 nm were simultaneously monitored. As shown in Fig. 6 , when the mixture of two targets with different concentrations were added into the dual-color probes and CNPs solution, the fluorescence intensity of bGQDs-PHIV probe increased as the concentration of target THIV increased from 0.05 nM to 10 nM, and that of gGQDs-PHBV probe also increased with the increasing THBV concentration of 0.05 nM–14 nM, due to the hybridization between DNA probes and the corresponding targets and the subsequent release of double-helix strands from CNPs surfaces. The detection limit of target THIV was determined to be 43 pM calculated from the slope of the linearity range of 0.05–0.8 nM and standard deviation, and in a similar way the detection limit of target THBV was calculated to be 46 pM. Although the detection limits were not as low as that of single-target detection (Figs. 4 and 5), this EXO III-assisted dual-color probes biosensing platform still presented almost 10 folds lower detection limits compared with the previous traditional unamplified strategy. All the above results demonstrated the proposed dual-color GQDs-ssDNA probes and CNPs nanoplatform can be applied to the simultaneous detection of multiplex targets with high sensitivity due to the EXO III-assisted signal amplification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The selectivity and anti-interference ability of the GQDs-ssDNA probes and CNPs biosensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: Besides sensitivity, another important parameter to evaluate the performance of the fluorescent biosensing platform is selectivity. We also investigate the selectivity of this GQDs-ssDNA probes and CNPs system by comparing the fluorescence recovery induced by single-base mismatched targets or noncomplementary oligonucleotides with fully complementary target THIV and THBV. As shown in Fig. 7 A, the addition of the same concentration of noncomplementary oligonucleotides (rDNA) or the single-base mismatched targets (MHIV) showed negligible fluorescence response or only a very weak fluorescence recovery of less than 10%, compared to the maximum fluorescence recovery caused by fully complementary targets (THIV) in PBS buffer. We also observed the similar results in the selectivity experiments of HBV detection system (cyan histogram of Fig. 7B), and all the above results demonstrated the good discrimination ability of this dual-color probes system to single-base mismatched targets. In addition, to assess the feasibility and anti-interference ability in practical application, we accomplished the detection experiments of single-base mismatched and perfect complementary targets in an imitated biological environment with 1% human serum spiked. As shown in the purple histogram of Fig. 7, the results obtained from 1% human serum samples are similar to that of PBS system, which indicates that this biosensing system has potential to discriminate perfectly complementary targets from mismatched DNA targets in real biological sample analysis with good anti-interference ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, we have successfully developed a highly sensitive multiplex detection strategy by combining dual-color GQDs-ssDNA probes and nonfluorescence CNPs quencher with EXO III-assisted target recycling and signal amplification strategy. The Exo III-assisted sequence-independent recycling of multiple targets DNA is an excellent method to simultaneously amplify multiple fluorescence signals caused by various targets to realize highly sensitive detection at lower concentration ranges. This dual-color GQDs-ssDNA probes and CNPs system with the assistance of Exo III signal amplification strategy provides a significant platform for respective detection and simultaneous detection of multiple targets (THIV and THBV), with the detection limit as low as 6.6 pM and 9.5 pM, presenting good discrimination ability to perfectly matched target DNA and single-base mismatched targets in both HIV and HBV detection systems. We believe that combined with multiple-colored GQDs, this Exo III signal amplification method-assisted GQDs-ssDNA probes strategy has the potential to be a versatile tool for highly sensitive and selective detection of other multiple-targets systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
ce:section.ce:para: Song Wang: Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Data analysis, Writing – original draft. Guangjie Kang: Data curation, Validation, Visualization, Resources. Fangli Cui: Validation, Data analysis. Yingwei Zhang: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Data curation, Investigation, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the support from National Natural Science Fundation of China (grants 52072024, 51672022, 51302010).
